summary: Jeff Atwood argues that traditional password composition rules (requiring uppercase, lowercase, numbers, special characters) are counterproductive and should be abandoned in favor of a single, simple rule: length. He demonstrates that complex rules frustrate power users with password managers, encourage average users to create weaker passwords through 'creative' workarounds, and fail to account for Unicode and international users. Instead, he advocates for minimum length requirements, checking against common breached password lists, basic entropy validation, and blocking obvious cases like password matching username. The post references NIST's 2016 recommendation against composition rules and provides data showing that simply requiring 10+ characters eliminates 80% of the most common passwords.
key_points: Traditional password composition rules (uppercase, lowercase, numbers, symbols) don't actually improve security and actively harm usability for both power users and average users
Password length is the single most important factor - an 8-character password is 'perilously close to no password at all' with modern GPU cracking
Checking passwords against lists of commonly breached passwords (top 10k-100k) is far more effective than arbitrary composition rules
Unicode support matters - password rules and common password lists are often ASCII-centric and fail international users
Simple post-entry validation (blocking passwords equal to username/email, checking basic entropy) catches edge cases without burdening users with complex upfront rules
money_quotes: These days, given the state of cloud computing and GPU password hash cracking, any password of 8 characters or less is perilously close to no password at all.
They heavily penalize your ideal audience, people that use real random password generators.
They frustrate average users, who then become uncooperative and use 'creative' workarounds that make their passwords less secure.
If you don't allow (almost) every single Unicode character in the password input field, you are probably doing it wrong.

There's no question that a hacker will submit these common passwords in a hack attempt – and it's shocking how far you can get, even with aggressive password attempt rate limiting, using just the 1,000 most common passwords.
These aren't hypothetical synthetic Jack the Ripper password rules some bored programmer dreamed up, these are real passwords used by real users.
In short, try to think outside the password input box, like a user would.
key_insight: Replace complex password composition rules with a single requirement—sufficient length—supplemented by silent checks against known breached passwords, because length provides real security while composition rules just create frustration and false confidence.
